message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="steve-krug" color="red" summary="Design decisions affecting accessibility: edit and delete">
Heydon, heads up — Steve Schoger and I just worked through some component design decisions. Two that directly affect accessibility:

**Delete button: always visible, visually quiet.** I pushed back on hover-to-reveal. The delete affordance will be a small muted icon on every todo item, always visible. This is better for keyboard and screen reader users too — no need to discover a hidden action.

**Edit: visible pencil icon rather than click-to-edit.** Steve originally proposed clicking the todo title to enter edit mode. I flagged that this is not discoverable for most users. We're going with a visible pencil icon instead. This is also much better for you — a clear, focusable button with a label like "Edit [todo title]" that screen readers can announce. Click-to-edit on a text element would be a nightmare for assistive tech.

Both the edit and delete buttons should be proper `<button>` elements with accessible labels. The icons are visual affordances; the labels are for everyone who can't see them.

Wanted to make sure you're aligned before the team starts building these components.
</teammate-message>

content.text: Steve, this is exactly what I've been advocating. Let me confirm alignment and add the implementation specifics.

input.content:
Steve, completely aligned on both decisions. You and Schoger have made the right calls here.

**Delete: always visible, visually quiet -- perfect.**
Hover-to-reveal is a discoverability failure for everyone (mouse users don't know to hover, keyboard users never trigger it, screen reader users can't discover it at all). A muted icon that's always present is honest UI -- the affordance exists, it's findable, and it doesn't demand attention until you need it.

Implementation requirements for the delete button:
```html
<button type="button"
        aria-label="Delete {{ title }}"
        hx-delete="/todos/{{ id }}"
        hx-target="closest .todo-item"
        hx-swap="outerHTML"
        hx-confirm="Delete this todo?"
        class="todo-action todo-action--delete">
  <!-- SVG icon or CSS icon here -->
</button>
```

Key points:
- `aria-label="Delete {{ title }}"` -- the screen reader announces "Delete Buy milk, button." Without the title, it would just say "button" or "Delete, button" -- and if you have 10 items, you'd hear "Delete, button" ten times with no way to know which one is which.
- The icon is purely decorative, so it gets `aria-hidden="true"` (or is a CSS pseudo-element/background).
- `type="button"` not `type="submit"` -- this is an action, not a form submission.

**Edit: visible pencil icon -- equally perfect.**
Click-to-edit on text is the UI equivalent of a trap door. You only find it by falling through it. A visible button that says "I am the edit action" respects every user.

Implementation:
```html
<button type="button"
        aria-label="Edit {{ title }}"
        hx-get="/todos/{{ id }}/edit"
        hx-target="closest .todo-item"
        hx-swap="outerHTML"
        class="todo-action todo-action--edit">
  <!-- pencil icon -->
</button>
```

Same `aria-label` pattern -- "Edit Buy milk, button." Focusable, announced correctly, discoverable by everyone.

**Tab order within a todo item:**
With checkbox + label + edit button + delete button, the tab order through a single todo item is:
1. Checkbox (toggle complete)
2. Edit button
3. Delete button

That's three tab stops per item. For a list of 10 items, that's 30 tab stops, which is a lot. This is fine for now (MVP), but if it becomes an issue later, we could consider a roving tabindex pattern where you Tab to the item, then use arrow keys within it. That's a v2 optimization though -- don't design it, don't implement it.

**One more thing**: both buttons should have consistent visual sizing (touch target minimum 44x44 CSS pixels per WCAG 2.5.8, or at least 24x24 with sufficient spacing). Even if the icon is small, the clickable/tappable area should be generous. This is especially important on mobile.

We're fully aligned. Good work pushing back on click-to-edit -- "Don't Make Me Think" extends to "Don't Make Me Guess What's Clickable."
input.summary: Fully aligned on delete and edit patterns with a11y details

content.text:
Good, Steve and I are locked in. The usability-accessibility alliance is working exactly as planned -- he catches discoverability issues, I provide the implementation details to make it work for assistive technology, and we present a unified recommendation.

The key decisions confirmed:
- **Delete**: Always-visible muted icon button, `aria-label="Delete {{ title }}"`, proper `<button>` element
- **Edit**: Visible pencil icon button, `aria-label="Edit {{ title }}"`, proper `<button>` element, replaces click-to-edit
- **Both**: 44x44px minimum touch target, decorative icons with `aria-hidden`, `type="button"`

These are the patterns the mob will use when building Tasks #7 (Delete), #8 (Complete -- already using checkbox), and #9 (Edit). The accessibility requirements for every todo item interaction are now defined and agreed between the UX specialist, UI designer, and accessibility specialist before a single line of component code is written. That's inclusive design from the start.
